title: Dissipation-assisted preparation of Floquet-Laughlin states in superconducting circuits
authors: Luis C. Steinfadt, André Eckardt, Francesco Petiziol
abstract: Fractional Chern insulators (FCIs) are lattice analogs of fractional quantum Hall systems, where the interplay of strong interactions with a frustrated tunnelling kinetics leads to the emergence of a gapped ground state with long-range entanglement and anyonic excitations. The highly correlated nature of such systems makes their adiabatic preparation challenging already beyond the minimal system size of two particles. Considering Floquet implementations of the bosonic Harper-Hofstadter-Hubbard model of few photons in superconducting circuits, we design protocols for the driven-dissipative stabilization of its FCI ground state at half filling via quantum bath engineering. Dissipation control is achieved through the coupling to driven leaky cavity modes, which realize a tuneable artificial environment having the Floquet-FCI as its approximate fixed point. For systems of two, three and six particles, we show numerically how the flexibility of the control scheme further allows for the detection of fractional quantum Hall signatures in the stabilized steady states, including bulk incompressibility, Hall response and the trapping of fractional charges. Our results provide a concrete pathway to dissipation-assisted preparation of strongly correlated states in quantum simulators.
summary: This paper proposes a method to prepare fractional Chern insulator states in superconducting circuits by using engineered dissipation to drive the system into a target ground state. By coupling a Floquet-engineered lattice to leaky cavities, the researchers show that the Laughlin-like state can be stabilized and its topological signatures, such as fractional charge pinning, can be detected.
main_problem: The difficulty of preparing and stabilizing highly correlated Fractional Chern Insulator (FCI) states, such as the Laughlin state, using traditional adiabatic protocols due to phase transition gaps and excitations.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a protocol for the autonomous, dissipation-assisted stabilization of Floquet-Laughlin states in superconducting circuits, achieving ground-state fidelities above 85% and observing signatures like Hall response and fractional charge pinning.
method: Quantum bath engineering using driven-dissipative coupling to leaky cavity modes, modeled via Lindblad master equations, Pauli rate equations, and numerical techniques like DMRG and quantum trajectories.
model_or_system: A Floquet-engineered Harper-Hofstadter-Bose-Hubbard model of hard-core bosons implemented in superconducting circuits (circuit QED), where lattice sites are coupled to driven, leaky microwave resonators.
key_observables: Ground-state fidelity, Hall conductivity (Streda formula), bulk incompressibility, and the pinning of fractional charges.
important_parameters: High-frequency Floquet regime (hbar omega >> J), bad-cavity regime, dispersive atom-cavity coupling, cavity detunings, and decay rates.
main_assumptions: Neglects transmon dephasing and spontaneous decay; assumes the resonator decay is the dominant dissipative rate; assumes the validity of the dispersive approximation by avoiding accidental resonances.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows stabilization fidelity over time and Hall conductivity for a two-particle system; Figure 2 illustrates bulk charge response to local potential dips and the effects of varying cavity coupling and detuning.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of FCI preparation, defines the Floquet-engineered superconducting platform, derives the effective dissipative dynamics via perturbation theory, presents numerical results for various particle numbers (N=2, 3, 6), and concludes with the robustness of the scheme.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete pathway for using reservoir engineering and dissipation to prepare non-equilibrium topological states, bridging the gap between open quantum systems and many-body physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Driven-dissipative stabilization of the ground state of the L = 4 HHBH lattice with N = 2 at quarter flux, ϕ = π/2, starting from an infinite temperature state. A fidelity of 85% is reached using four cavities. Inset: Lattice geometry with artificial atoms and cavities marked by blue and purple bullets, respectively. The solution of Eq. (2) (solid lines) and Eq. (4) (dashed) show good agreement. (b) The stabilization time can be decreased to less than 800 ℏ/J, when starting from a superposition of the five lowest energy eigenstates, and to less than 200 ℏ/J (inset) by further adding twelve identical cavities, coupled to symmetrically chosen lattice sites. (c) The bulk density of...

FIG. 2. Change of the bulk charge, defined as the average particle number in the inner L −1 × L −1 sites, in response to a local potential dip of −V on four sites the center of a system of N = 3 and L = 6 (a) or N = 6 and L = 8 (b). For each value of V , the system is initialized in a mixture of low- energy eigenstates and stabilized to the ground state, reaching a fidelity of around 80%. The change in the total charge in the bulk compared to the unperturbed state was computed (cf. inset of (a) for the L = 6 lattice, analog for L = 8).
